elements.sent-2411-001: Polynomial reproduction plays a relevant role in deriving error estimates for various approximation schemes.
elements.sent-2411-002: Local reproduction in a quasi-uniform setting is a significant factor in the estimation of error and the assessment of stability but for some computationally relevant schemes, such as Rescaled Localized Radial Basis Functions (RL-RBF), it becomes a limitation.
elements.sent-2411-003: To facilitate the study of a greater variety of approximation methods in a unified and efficient manner, this work proposes a framework based on fast decaying polynomial reproduction: we do not restrict to compactly supported basis functions, but we allow the basis function decay to infinity as a function of the {\it separation distance .}
elements.sent-2411-004: Implementing fast decaying polynomial reproduction provides stable and convergent methods, that can be smooth when approximating by moving least squares otherwise, it is very efficient in the case of linear programming problems.
elements.sent-2411-005: All the results presented in this paper concerning the rate of convergence, the Lebesgue constant, the smoothness of the approximant, and the compactness of the support have been verified numerically, even in the multivariate setting.
elements.sent-2411-006: Polynomials are widely utilized in approximation theory due to their ease of definition, implementation, and immediate error analysis by resorting to Taylor expansion.

elements.sent-2411-009: In \cite{wendland_2001 , in the case of radial basis functions with compact support, a bound for the Lebesgue constants, which is uniform and independent of the space dimension, is provided.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The insights of certain arguments in \cite{demarchi2020 enable us to reformulate the structure of polynomial reproduction in a general context.}
elements.sent-2411-012: It is possible to contextualize the Rescaled Localised Radial Basis Function method (RL-RBF), introduced in \cite{deparis_2014 , more effectively using the idea of fast decaying polynomial reproduction.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The convergence of the RL-RBF interpolant has been analyzed in \cite{demarchi2020 leaving open a conjecture on the sum of the cardinal functions related to the RL-RBF approximant.}
elements.sent-2411-014: This paper aims to provide a new approach to polynomial reproduction based on the assumption that the basis functions are only decaying to infinity.

elements.sent-2411-203: We modified the definition of local polynomial reproduction by replacing the compactness of the support with a fast decay of the basis functions.
elements.sent-2411-204: The RL-RBF method adapts to this new definition, while it does not reproduce polynomials locally because the cardinal functions do not have compact support.
elements.sent-2411-205: In the quasi-uniform setting this new approximation scheme is convergent and stable (the Lebesgue constant depends on the dimension of the space, the dimension of the polynomial space that is reproduced and on the decaying of the basis functions).
elements.sent-2411-206: At this point in the analysis, smoothness plays no fundamental role.
elements.sent-2411-207: In addition to RL-RBF method, we proposed a further approximation scheme that provides smooth quasi-interpolants.
elements.sent-2411-208: The method approximates the value of an unknown function using the moving least squares technique with a Gaussian as weight function.
elements.sent-2411-209: The smoothness of the interpolant is inherited from the smoothness of the weight functions.
elements.sent-2411-210: Since the solution of a moving least squares problem is the solution of a quadratic optimization problem we can analyze the computational cost of the method, which turns out to be linear in the number of nodes.
elements.sent-2411-211: Numerical tests confirm the theoretical results of convergence, stability and also the smoothness of the basis functions.
elements.sent-2411-212: With the numerical tests we obtain an unexpected result: even if the weight function is only continuous, if we increase the dimension of the polynomial space to be reproduced then the basis functions turn out to be numerically smooth.
elements.sent-2411-213: In these analysis and numerical tests we considered functions with global support and the matrices involved can be dense although small in size when the space to be reproduced is not too large.
elements.sent-2411-214: To address this difficulty we replaced the quadratic optimization problem deriving from moving least squares with a linear program on a polyhedron.
elements.sent-2411-215: Also in this area, with techniques similar to the previous ones, a stable and convergent method can be achieved with the same convergence rate.
elements.sent-2411-216: A vertex solution of the linear optimization problem allows us to control the number of non-zero basis functions at each point in the domain.
elements.sent-2411-217: Since the weight functions try to locate the optimization problem, we expect that the value of the weight function corresponding to a node in the domain is large when the considered point is far from the node.
elements.sent-2411-218: This type of experience leads us to use column generation techniques to reduce the dimensionality of the problem (we can try to predict the non-zero basis functions because the number of them is bounded uniformly with respect to the fill distance).
elements.sent-2411-219: The numerical results confirm the theoretical evidence and even if we do not have any results on the smoothness of the approximant we get similar outcomes to the moving least squares method (numerically we can observe that the basis functions become smooth when the polynomial space is reproduced gets bigger).
elements.sent-2411-220: The methods have also been subjected to multivariate testing.
elements.sent-2411-221: The numerical results confirm the framework's validity, but the exponent's choice is crucial to achieving stability and convergence.
elements.sent-2411-222: Another line of research refers to the optimality of the Lebesgue constant in equation \eqref{def_constant_K_stability .}
elements.sent-2411-223: Indeed, by tuning the parameters $C$ and $\varphi$ it is possible to reach better stability and obtain optimal controls for the decay of the basis functions.
